title: To a Trillion(s) Dollars and beyond: A SpaceX IPO Odyssey!
gist: Damodaran builds a pre-prospectus sum-of-the-parts DCF on SpaceX (launch, Starlink, xAI/Grok, plus expansion options) at an 8% cost of capital, arriving at $1.22 trillion versus a $1.25 trillion private-market price and a rumored $1.75 trillion IPO price, while arguing that pricing off peers is just as assumption-laden as valuation but hides its biases.

themes.theme: SpaceX's business mix and growth trajectory

insights.claim: SpaceX now operates three distinct core businesses (space launch, Starlink satellite internet, and xAI/Grok LLM) plus a set of expansion options, and Damodaran values each separately because they differ in operating metrics and competitive dynamics.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Investors should model SpaceX as a diversified conglomerate with three separate risk/growth profiles rather than a single aerospace comp, since a single blended multiple will misprice it.
insights.locator: To value SpaceX, I consider each of its three core businesses separately

insights.claim: SpaceX's revenue mix flipped dramatically from launch-dependent to Starlink-dependent: 2021 revenue was just over $2 billion, almost entirely from launch, but by 2025 estimated revenue reached $15.6 billion, with launch only ~30% ($4.1B) and Starlink about $11.4 billion.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The bull case for SpaceX now rests on Starlink's subscription economics, not on the launch business that built its reputation — competitive and margin risk in launch matters less to the equity story than it used to.
insights.quote: Starlink's subscriber based model has allowed revenues to increase more than five-fold since, reaching an estimated $15.6 billion in 2025
insights.locator: SpaceX had a slow start financially

insights.claim: xAI, acquired by SpaceX in February 2026, had only about $100 million in subscription revenue in 2025 (later cited as ~$80 million from X Premium bundling), making it immaterial to current financials despite driving much of the IPO excitement.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The AI narrative around SpaceX is priced well ahead of any revenue reality from xAI; investors are paying for optionality, not current cash flow.
insights.locator: xAI, which was acquired in 2026, had subscription revenues of roughly $100 million in 2025

insights.claim: Starlink held roughly two-thirds of all satellites in orbit (~10,000 of the global count) at end of 2025 and doubled its subscriber base to just over 10 million in the prior year, while Amazon's acquisition of GlobalStar introduces a competitor with only a fraction of Starlink's satellite count.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Starlink's infrastructure lead (satellite count) is currently so large that near-term competitive threats like Amazon/GlobalStar are unlikely to dent market share, supporting the high share assumptions in the valuation.
insights.locator: At the end of 2025, Starlink had close to 10,000 satellites in space

insights.claim: Satellite-based internet is still a niche relative to the ~$1.5 trillion global internet services market, capturing only about 1% (under $15 billion in revenue) in 2025 because broadband quality still lags fiber and cable outside of rural areas, disaster zones, and mobile transit use cases.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Starlink's total addressable market is currently much smaller than headline satellite hype implies; the bull case depends entirely on the forecast that satellite share of internet services rises from <1% to 10% by 2036.
insights.locator: the total internet service market is estimated to be close to a trillion and a half dollars globally

insights.claim: Grok/xAI trails Anthropic (Claude), OpenAI (ChatGPT), and Google (Gemini) in both usage and monetization, and Damodaran thinks it may deliberately concede the larger, more competitive business-application market to those rivals in favor of consumer subscriptions.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Investors should not underwrite SpaceX's LLM segment as a Claude/ChatGPT/Gemini competitor; the more realistic (and monetizable) path is a smaller, higher-margin consumer niche.
insights.quote: It is possible that Grok may concede the larger and more competitive business space to Claude and ChatGPT and focus instead on consumer subscriptions, giving it a smaller market, but one with less competition.
insights.locator: The Large Language Model (LLM) business

insights.claim: xAI's recent acquisition of Cursor, an AI coding startup, signals that xAI has not fully abandoned competing for business/enterprise AI applications despite lagging Anthropic, OpenAI and Google.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Watch xAI's M&A (like Cursor) as a leading indicator of whether it pivots back toward contesting enterprise AI share rather than staying consumer-only, which would change the margin/growth assumptions in any valuation.
insights.quote: The recent acquisition of Cursor, a young AI company in the coding space, suggests that xAI has not thrown in the towel on business applications.
insights.locator: The recent acquisition of Cursor

themes.theme: Financials, funding history, and ownership structure

insights.claim: SpaceX's estimated $8 billion EBITDA in 2025 masks unclear operating or net profitability once depreciation and other expenses are factored in, because full financial statements are not yet public.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Any pre-IPO multiple based on EBITDA overstates true profitability quality; investors should discount reported EBITDA until GAAP operating/net income is disclosed in the prospectus.
insights.locator: it is estimated that SpaceX generated an EBITDA of $8 billion in 2025

insights.claim: SpaceX raised over $12 billion across more than thirty venture rounds since a $12 million first round in 2002, funded initially by $100 million of Musk's own PayPal proceeds, with backers including Founders Fund, Andreessen Horowitz, Sequoia, Fidelity, and Google.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The long, capital-intensive private funding history explains why private-market pricing (~$1.25 trillion) already embeds sophisticated investor views, giving Damodaran's DCF a natural benchmark to test against.
insights.locator: The first venture capital round of about $12 million was in 2002

insights.claim: Despite dilution from many funding rounds, Elon Musk still owns about 42% of SpaceX's equity but, due to differential voting rights, controls close to 80% of total voting power.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Governance risk is structural and permanent: public shareholders will have limited influence over strategy regardless of the price they pay, a key risk factor for any IPO buyer.
insights.locator: he continues to own about 42% of the equity in the company, and with differential voting rights, close to 80% of the total voting rights

insights.claim: Damodaran argues that intrinsic (DCF) valuation does not require historical data or a peer group — only a business narrative — and that missing data makes estimates noisier, not impossible, directly rebutting the common claim that SpaceX 'cannot be valued.'
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Investors shouldn't wait for a prospectus to form a view; a narrative-driven DCF can be built now and refined later, giving early movers a valuation edge before consensus catches up.
insights.quote: the fact that you face uncertainty or that you are missing information does not imply that you cannot make estimates, just that the estimates will be noisier
insights.locator: It is true that intrinsic valuation

insights.claim: SpaceX's launch business has an 80%+ 2025 market share within a launch market estimated at $30 billion in 2026, forecast to grow to $100 billion by 2036, with Damodaran assuming share moderates to 70% and operating margins rise to 40% as costs fall with scale.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Even with share erosion baked in, the launch business alone implies roughly $28 billion of 2036 revenue at 40% margins — a benchmark investors can use to sanity-check bullish launch-segment assumptions.
insights.locator: The space launch market is estimated to be about $30 billion in 2026

insights.claim: Damodaran forecasts the satellite internet market growing from $15 billion to $160 billion by 2036 (rising from under 1% to 10% share of the total internet services market), with Starlink holding 75% share and operating margins approaching 60% as customer acquisition costs ease.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: This single assumption — satellite internet's addressable share rising 10x — does most of the heavy lifting in the valuation; if it doesn't play out, the $1.22 trillion figure has the most downside from this segment.
insights.locator: The satellite internet services market will continue to grow

insights.claim: Damodaran projects xAI/Grok revenue of $80 billion by 2036 at a 50% operating margin, deliberately smaller than LLM competitors because it avoids costly head-to-head competition with Anthropic, OpenAI and Google for business use.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The LLM segment's value comes from high assumed margin, not market leadership — a strategic bet that avoiding competition beats fighting for share, worth stress-testing given how volatile LLM economics have been.
insights.locator: xAI will target primarily consumer subscription revenues and niche business applications

insights.claim: Damodaran adds a separate 'expansion options' category (space travel, broadband expansion, xAI business applications) worth $50 billion of 2036 revenue at a 30% margin, with cash flows only starting after year 6 (2032) to reflect low current probability but high payoff.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: This is a real-options overlay on top of the core DCF — investors modeling SpaceX should treat this slice as the most speculative and least reliable component of the total value.
insights.locator: This part of the story is built on the expansion options

insights.claim: Damodaran uses an 8% cost of capital for SpaceX — close to the median for a US company — reasoning that most of SpaceX's risk is company-specific (diversifiable) and cuts both ways, with outliers more likely to skew upside than downside.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A surprisingly low discount rate for a young, uncertain company is doing real work to inflate the valuation; investors who view SpaceX's risk as non-diversifiable or downside-skewed should use a higher cost of capital and get a materially lower value.
insights.quote: much of the risk here is specific to the company (thus reducing the effect in a diversified portfolio) and cuts in both directions (upside and downside)
insights.locator: If you are wondering why the cost of capital is only 8%

themes.theme: Valuation output and market pricing gap

insights.claim: Damodaran's base-case intrinsic value for SpaceX is $1.22 trillion — about 10% below the private-market price (~$1.25 trillion) and roughly a third below the rumored IPO pricing ($1.75 trillion) — for a company with $15.5 billion of trailing revenue.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The base case reads as fair-to-slightly-rich versus the private market but meaningfully overvalued versus the rumored IPO price — a concrete anchor for judging where the IPO should be bought or avoided.
insights.quote: the value that I derive is $1.22 trillion, about 10% below the private market pricing and about a third below the expected IPO pricing
insights.locator: With my story and inputs

insights.claim: A Monte Carlo simulation across 10,000 trials centered on the same base-case inputs produces a median value of $1.29 trillion, and prices of $1.75-2 trillion fall within the simulated distribution but leave 'little or no upside' for an investor paying that much.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The rumored IPO price isn't statistically impossible, but buying at $1.75-2 trillion means paying for the high end of the distribution with asymmetric downside — a clear 'don't chase' signal.
insights.quote: a $1.75 trillion or even a $2 trillion pricing falls in the range of the distribution, though with little or no upside left for an investor paying that price
insights.locator: Since the simulation is centered on the same expected values

insights.claim: Damodaran predicts the IPO prospectus itself won't move the intrinsic value story because reported historical numbers will be too small relative to the value driven by future growth expectations, and warns the prospectus's market-size claims will be 'hype' with trillions of dollars thrown around for TAM.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Investors should treat any SpaceX prospectus TAM figures skeptically and build valuations before the roadshow narrative anchors expectations, rather than waiting for 'more information' that won't actually change the analysis.
insights.quote: expect to see trillions of dollars thrown around nonchalantly for market size
insights.locator: it is not an either/or proposition

themes.theme: Why pricing SpaceX is as flawed as valuing it

insights.claim: Using 2025 trailing revenue and EBITDA against the $1.25 trillion private-market equity value (assuming near-zero net debt) yields 'nosebleed' multiples of 81x revenue and 156x EBITDA.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Anyone justifying the IPO price on trailing multiples is not doing serious analysis; multiples this extreme only make sense if you buy the decade-out growth story, which should be made explicit rather than hidden behind a scalar.
insights.quote: the resulting enterprise value of $1.25 trillion yields nosebleed multiples of 81 times revenues and 156 times EBITDA
insights.locator: the absence of debt and cash numbers makes it impossible

insights.claim: Switching from trailing to forward (2030/2035) revenue collapses the EV/Sales multiple from roughly 80x/112x down to about 3.9x/5.5x at the private and IPO pricing levels, which Damodaran warns bullish analysts will exploit by inflating future revenue forecasts to make sky-high prices look reasonable.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A reasonable-looking forward multiple can be manufactured simply by picking an aggressive enough revenue forecast — investors should demand to see the growth assumptions behind any forward multiple pitched to them, not just the multiple itself.
insights.quote: it is inevitable that bullish analysts, unable to justify the sky high pricing values with trailing 12-month numbers, will resort to using forward pricing, and add to the bias, by inflating revenues in future years
insights.locator: There is no inherent problem with using forward numbers

insights.claim: There is no valid peer group for pricing SpaceX: Boeing and Northrop Grumman are too low-growth/low-margin for the launch business, Palantir lacks SpaceX's infrastructure intensity, Verizon and T-Mobile aren't growth comparables for Starlink, and LLM peers are mostly private companies priced on small-sample VC rounds.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Any relative-value pricing analysts publish for SpaceX will necessarily use cherry-picked, imperfect comps; treat 'comparable company' multiples in sell-side notes as narrative dressing rather than rigorous benchmarking.
insights.locator: The even bigger challenge in pricing SpaceX

insights.claim: Damodaran predicts there will be far more buy than sell/hold recommendations when SpaceX goes public, with analysts using forward multiples, hand-picked peer groups, and unsubstantiated growth stories ('fairy tales') as ex-post rationalization rather than genuine analysis.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Expect sell-side coverage to be structurally biased bullish; investors should discount the ratio of buy ratings and look instead at the underlying assumptions each analyst uses.
insights.quote: I will predict that there will be far more buy than sell or hold recommendations for SpaceX, when it does go public, with analysts doing pricing gymnastics with forward multiples, hand-picked peer groups and fairy tales to justifying their positions
insights.locator: No matter what peer group you pick

insights.claim: The core distinction Damodaran draws is that intrinsic valuation forces explicit judgments about revenue, margins and reinvestment, while multiple-based pricing lets the same biased assumptions remain implicit and unexamined.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: When evaluating any SpaceX price target, investors should reverse-engineer the implied growth/margin assumptions behind the multiple rather than accepting the multiple at face value.
insights.quote: the only difference between intrinsic valuation and pricing...is that with the former...you have to face up to the uncertainties and make your best explicit judgments...whereas with the latter...they remain implicit
insights.locator: Put simply, the only difference

themes.theme: The Musk factor and investment stance

insights.claim: Damodaran expects SpaceX to behave like Tesla under Musk's control: a 'shape shifting entity' that shifts narrative unpredictably rather than following a linear corporate life cycle, causing 'whiplash' for investors expecting orderly growth-to-maturity progression.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Standard valuation frameworks that assume a stable maturing narrative will repeatedly be invalidated by sudden strategic pivots (as with the xAI and Cursor acquisitions); investors need to revisit the model frequently rather than set-and-forget.
insights.quote: it will shift from one narrative to another, often with no advance warning, causing whiplash for investors
insights.locator: SpaceX is an engineering marvel

insights.claim: Damodaran frames SpaceX as hard to buy but even more dangerous to sell short, citing Tesla short sellers' experience over the last two decades as the cautionary precedent.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Asymmetric risk favors staying out or going long selectively rather than shorting overvaluation — the same narrative unpredictability that creates valuation risk for longs has repeatedly destroyed short positions in Musk companies.
insights.quote: That makes it a difficult company to buy, but it makes it an even more dangerous company to sell short, as Tesla short sellers have discovered in the last two decades
insights.locator: When I bought Tesla in 2019

insights.claim: Damodaran states he would not buy SpaceX at the rumored $1.75 trillion IPO price but would become a buyer after 'one big correction,' since he views the company as having immense competitive advantages despite the governance baggage of its founder.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A concrete, actionable stance: wait for a post-IPO drawdown rather than buying the offering, consistent with the DCF ($1.22T) sitting well below the rumored IPO price ($1.75T).
insights.quote: while I would not be interested in buying at the rumored IPO pricing of $1.75 trillion, it is one big correction away from being fairly priced or even cheap. If that happens, I will be a buyer
insights.locator: With all that said, SpaceX is a unique company
